sub: How AI agents work and why architecture matters

body.card: You’ve used Claude Code for 6 modules. Now understand how it actually works.
items: Understanding architecture helps you use tools better
Security decisions require architectural understanding
You’ll encounter (and maybe build) agent systems in your career
You already know most of these patterns — you just need the vocabulary

rows: Who changed what, when, and why
Declares exactly what a system can do
Every step is visible and measurable
Humans approve before risky actions execute
Sensitive data never leaves the perimeter
table.note: You’ve been learning production agent architecture for 6 modules — without realizing it.

title: Claude Code: The Six Key Abstractions
desc: Everything in Claude Code exists to support these six core building blocks.

rows: Streams model response, collects tool calls, executes them, loops
Single heartbeat for REPL, SDK, sub-agents, and headless mode
Read, edit, run shell, search — anything the agent can do
Tools carry their own permissions, concurrency rules, and UI rendering
Recursive capability: agents delegate to sub-agents
Session config (mutable singleton) + UI state (reactive store)
Infrastructure state vs. render state — separated by design
Lifecycle interceptors at PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop events
Deterministic guards — can block tool calls before permission prompt
How Claude Code remembers conventions and decisions across sessions

title: Four Guiding Principles
desc: Four rules that guide every production AI agent platform.

items: 1. Control Before Autonomy — First construct predictable paths; then expand agent freedom.
2. Security Can’t Be Sidelined — If policy and approvals aren’t embedded in runtime, architecture fixes become emergencies.
3. State Must Be Explicit — Long tasks shouldn’t lose steps because someone restarted the process.
4. Observability Over Impressiveness — If agents “appear intelligent” but lack traces, the system isn’t managed.

title: Memory Architecture: Three Layers
desc: Different types of knowledge need different storage and trust levels.

body.quote: “Memory write path must be treated as security-sensitive, not a technical detail. Errors in memory live longer than errors in single responses.”

title: MCP: Standard Protocol for Agent-Tool Communication

body.code:
Agent <--MCP--> File system server
Agent <--MCP--> Git server  
Agent <--MCP--> Database server
Agent <--MCP--> Any tool with MCP adapter

items: Think of MCP like USB for AI tools: one standard plug for any tool
Without standards, every AI tool reinvents file reading, command execution, and search
MCP makes tools reusable

body.code: // .mcp.json — project-level (shared in Git) { "mcpServers": { "github": { "command": "npx", "args": ["@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"] } } } // ~/.claude.json — personal (NOT in Git) // API keys, personal tool connections
body.note: Project tools go in .mcp.json (shared). Personal credentials go in ~/.claude.json (private).

items: 4–5 tools per agent maximum — more tools = worse tool selection
Tool descriptions are critical: include input formats, examples, boundaries
Tell the tool when NOT to be used — negative constraints matter

desc: Model Context Protocol — the standard for agent-tool communication.

title: MCP for Tools, A2A for Agents
desc: Two emerging protocols for two different problems.

rows: USB — one plug for any device
Email — different people, different skills, collaborate on a task
You need to connect to APIs, databases, file systems
You need to delegate work to a specialized agent that reasons independently
Coding agent asks a security-review agent to audit its output

items: If it executes a command and returns a result → it’s a tool → use MCP
If it reasons, plans, and makes its own decisions → it’s an agent → use A2A
A database connector does not “think” — MCP. A code-review bot that decides what to flag — A2A.

title: Coordinator vs. Handoff Patterns
desc: When multiple agents collaborate, who is in charge?

body.card: 🎯 Coordinator pattern — one manager, many specialists
items: A central agent receives the task, splits it, delegates to specialists, and assembles the final result
Specialists report back — they never talk to each other directly
Real-world analogy: a project manager assigns tasks to developers, testers, and designers, then combines their work
Example: Claude Code spawns sub-agents (Explore, Plan, test-runner) — each does one job and reports back
Agent A finishes its part and transfers full control to Agent B, which then transfers to Agent C
No central manager — each agent owns the task while it holds the baton
Real-world analogy: a support ticket escalation: L1 support → L2 engineer → L3 specialist
Example: coding agent writes code → hands off to review agent → hands off to deploy agent

rows: ✅ Each agent owns its domain fully
Sequential pipeline (A then B then C)

body.code: PROJECT-COORDINATOR (Hub) / | | \ solution code-std test research architect reviewer spec analyst | | | | Designs Reviews Tests Researches workflow standards quality externals Each subagent has isolated context. No automatic inheritance from coordinator. Returns summary, not full output.
body.note: cognitive-core implements this: 10 specialist agents, each with scoped tools and instructions.

title: When AI Agent vs. When Simple Automation

body.quote: “Use workflows when execution paths are known. Use agents when the system must make non-obvious decisions.”

title: Observability: Seeing What the Agent Did

body.code:
Run #1234
  |— Step 1: Read Calculator.java         (2ms)
  |— Step 2: Analyze code structure        (model, 800ms)
  |— Step 3: Generate test cases           (model, 1200ms)
  |— Step 4: Write CalculatorTest.java     (5ms)
  |— Step 5: Run mvn test                  (3400ms) — SUCCESS
  Total: 5407ms | 2 model calls | 1800 tokens

body.quote: Without traces, agent systems are black boxes. Same as Jenkins (Module 5): you need to see each step, its duration, and its outcome.

title: The Production Launch Checklist
desc: Seven checks every system must pass before production.

items: 1. Runtime correctness — does the happy path work?
2. Safety and policy — are guardrails in place?
3. Capability readiness — does each tool have an owner?
4. Observability — can you see what happened?
5. Eval readiness — do tests cover critical scenarios?
6. Operational readiness — who’s on call when it breaks?
7. Ownership and rollback — can you undo a bad release?

body.quote: Compare to your capstone project (Module 8): does your project satisfy all 7?

title: The Parsimony Principle: KISS and YAGNI
desc: Prefer the simplest solution that meets requirements. Justify every added complexity.

body.code: User: I need audit trails for grade changes. Simple (parsimony): CRUD operations + append-only audit table. Meets all requirements. Minimal complexity. Complex (premature): Event Sourcing with temporal queries, replay, projection rebuilds, saga orchestration. Not requested. Not needed. Not now. Verdict: Start simple. Migrate to Event Sourcing only if future features require it.
body.note: Anthropic’s own guidance: “Find the simplest solution possible, and only increase complexity when needed.” cognitive-core enforces this in every agent.

body.card: You already know this under other names
items: KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid) — don’t overcomplicate what can be straightforward
YAGNI (You Aren’t Gonna Need It) — don’t build features until they’re actually required
Parsimony combines both: start with the simplest solution, add complexity only when you have evidence it’s needed
Agents: activate only the specialists a task requires — not every agent for every task
Tools: request only tools the task cannot work without (4–5 max)
Code: three similar lines > a premature abstraction
AI prompts: ask for exactly what you need — vague prompts produce over-engineered solutions
Exception: security uses defense-in-depth — parsimony within layers, not across them

body.code: Probabilistic (prompt-based): CLAUDE.md: "Never use System.out.println()" Reality: AI might follow this. Or might not. Deterministic (hook/gate-based): post-edit-lint.sh: runs mvn checkstyle:check Reality: violation is always caught. Cannot be bypassed.
body.note: Production systems use both: prompts for guidance, gates for enforcement.

items: Lint gate: 60% — basic formatting
Commit gate: 80% — standards + architecture
Test gate: 85% — coverage + quality
Merge gate: 90% — full review
Deploy gate: 95% — production readiness

body.code: You: Add a sqrt() method to Calculator with tests

body.card: Map each step to the architecture:
items: Which layer receives your input? (Interface)
Which layer decides what to do? (Cognition/orchestration)
Which layer reads Calculator.java? (Tool execution)
Which layer checks if it can write files? (Control plane)
Which layer writes the new code? (Tool execution)
Which layer runs the tests? (Tool execution)
Which layer shows you the result? (Interface)

title: Exercise 7.2: Design a Policy
desc: 15 minutes — think like a security architect

items: You are building an AI agent for a banking application
The agent can perform the actions listed below
Your job: assign a risk level and decide who must approve each action
Should the agent have a maximum number of steps per task? Why?
What data should the agent never store in its memory (e.g., passwords, account numbers)?
How does this compare to the Tool Gateway Pattern from Module 6?

body.quote: Compare your answers with a classmate. Did you agree on risk levels? Where did you disagree and why?

title: Exercise 7.3: Launch Checklist for Your Project

body.quote: How many blocks can you honestly mark as “ready”?

body.card: Compare all four quality layers on your project:
items: Layer 1: Run mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check manually — count violations
Layer 2: Edit a file and watch post-edit-lint hook fire automatically
Layer 3: Run /code-review skill — read the structured findings
Layer 4: Ask @code-standards-reviewer agent to review — compare depth

body.quote: Each layer adds intelligence. None replaces your judgment.

items: You’ve been learning production architecture patterns since Module 0
8 layers: interface, identity, control plane, orchestration, cognition, memory, tool execution, telemetry
Control plane = policies that restrict agent actions
MCP = standard for agent-tool communication; A2A = agent-to-agent
cognitive-core: hooks (reflexes) → skills (tools) → agents (reasoning) (see Module 11)
Fitness gates: deterministic enforcement (60→95%) beats hope-based prompts
Provenance: every finding needs a source (documented / verified / inferred / automated)
4 principles: control before autonomy, security embedded, explicit state, observability over impressiveness
